statement: The pace of EU legislative output has shifted markedly over the decades. In 1994–1998, the EU adopted an average of 2,907 regulations and 86 directives per year. By 2004–2008, this had fallen to 1,886 regulations and 112 directives per year. In the most recent period (2019–2023), annual output averaged 1,177 regulations and 48 directives. This declining trend in regulations reflects consolidation of the single market acquis, though recent years have seen major new legislative packages (Digital Services Act, AI Act, Green Deal regulations) that are individually more complex.
caveats: Fewer regulations does not necessarily mean less regulatory activity — modern regulations tend to be more complex and far-reaching. Delegated and implementing acts (secondary legislation) are not captured in these headline counts but have grown significantly.

related_entries.statement: If Iceland were to join the EU, it would need to adopt the full acquis communautaire. The EUR-Lex database records over 151,000 binding legal acts (regulations and directives combined) adopted since 1958, though many have been repealed or superseded. During Iceland's 2009–2015 accession negotiations, the screening process covered 33 chapters. Iceland had already adopted approximately one-third of the acquis through the EEA Agreement, principally in internal market areas, but would need to incorporate legislation in areas outside the EEA including fisheries policy (CFP), agriculture (CAP), monetary policy, and justice/home affairs.

Chapter 13 (Fisheries) was the most contentious chapter during Iceland's accession negotiations. The EU's Common Fisheries Policy requires member states to share fishing quotas under the principle of equal access to EU waters. Iceland's fisheries sector accounted for approximately 40% of goods exports and 5–6% of GDP at the time, and the Icelandic ITQ (Individual Transferable Quota) system was considered incompatible with CFP principles without significant adaptation.
